proposed_from.topic: Sharp partial identification of the LATE with an ordinal instrument under bounded per-rung defier proportions, extending Imbens-Angrist monotonicity to a calibrated partial-defiance regime where the share of defiers is observably restricted on each adjacent IV-rung pair
proposed_from.novelty_justification: {"repo_axis":"Closest in-catalogue prior art remains doc/API.md §3 for the ThmSmith PartialID staging area, the banked binary proposal pid_late_bounded_defiers/one_sided_compliance, and pid_dynamic_iv_compliance_v2 as a latent response-map sharpness analogue. The gap is unchanged in v3: none of these catalogue entries has an ordinal IV response path where 010/101 link two adjacent bounded-defier problems and can make pairwise sharp bounds non-pasteable.","published_axis":"Theorem 1 is explicitly a known monotone benchmark from ImbensAngrist1994 and AngristImbens1995, and Theorem 2 is routine BalkePearl1997-style finite response-type sharpness recast for ordinal paths. The novelty claim is Conjecture 1 and its ray-indexed Conjecture 2: HuberMellace2012 is now characterized as binary-IV local monotonicity by outcome-support regions, while SunWuthrich2025 is the closest pairwise valid-instrument comparator but estimates valid-pair LATEs rather than sharp global ordinal response-path bounds under bounded adjacent defier caps."}
seed_list: Prior work AngristImbens1995 would predict an ordinal-IV estimand remains a weighted adjacent-rung causal response under ordered monotonicity. Our seed conjectures that with bounded per-rung defiers, adjacent binary-IV bounds are not pasteable because zig-zag paths share one treatment effect with opposite signs across neighboring reduced forms.

Prior work Noack2021 parameterizes binary LATE monotonicity violations by defier share and outcome-distribution sensitivity. Our seed conjectures that ordinal per-rung defier caps alone can tighten the identified set because the same latent response path must satisfy all adjacent rungs simultaneously.

Prior work BalkePearl1997 establishes sharp binary-IV bounds through a latent response-type LP. Our seed conjectures that the ordinal bounded-defier LP has new cross-rung facets because response paths 010 and 101 are impossible to decompose into independent binary principal strata.

Prior work deChaisemartin2017 would imply defiers can be tolerated under compliers-defiers cancellation. Our seed conjectures that without cancellation, ordinal zig-zag paths generate partial-ID tightening rather than point identification because cancellation must be globally path-compatible.

Prior work DahlHuberMellace2023 identifies LATEs under local monotonicity/local CD assumptions. Our seed conjectures that weaker per-rung defier caps yield a sharp interval with an ordinal kink because local defier roles overlap across adjacent pairs.

Prior work MogstadTorgovitskyWalters2021 studies positive 2SLS weights under partial monotonicity with multiple instruments. Our seed conjectures that a single ordinal instrument has an analogous but distinct compatibility test because adjacent monotonicity slack must come from one ordered response path.

Prior work BaiHuangMoonShaikhVytlacil2024 would predict generalized monotonicity may add no ATE identifying power. Our seed conjectures a latent-type LATE target behaves differently because the target denominator counts up-complier events whose path compatibility is restricted by defier caps.

Prior work HuberMellace2015 and Kitagawa2015 use inequality violations to test LATE assumptions. Our seed conjectures that adjacent-rung inequality slack can be converted into sharp LATE bound width because slack identifies how much zig-zag mass can enter the response-path polytope.

Prior work Vytlacil2002 equates monotonicity with a latent-index selection model. Our seed conjectures that bounded defier caps reveal the first finite violation of scalar-index order because 010 and 101 paths create a measurable ordinal incompatibility facet.

Prior work AngristImbensRubin1996 decomposes binary IV principal strata. Our seed conjectures that ordinal principal strata are not just repeated binary strata because the same unit can be both a complier and a defier on different adjacent rungs.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work AngristImbens1995 would predict an ordinal-IV estimand remains a weighted adjacent-rung causal response under ordered monotonicity. Our seed conjectures that with bounded per-rung defiers, adjacent binary-IV bounds are not pasteable because zig-zag paths share one treatment effect with opposite signs across neighboring reduced forms.

anchor_paper.author: Joshua D. Angrist and Guido W. Imbens
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Shows TSLS with variable treatment intensity identifies a weighted average of causal responses under ordered monotonicity.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Boundary/regime-shift pattern, not blocked: the new content is failure of pairwise pasting via zig-zag paths, not a routine weakening of monotonicity.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from AngristImbens1995 because per-rung monotonicity can be checked pairwise.
seed_details.rebuttal: Angrist-Imbens assumes no adjacent decreases; with bounded decreases, the same 010/101 response path enters two adjacent reduced forms with a shared treatment effect, which pairwise checks ignore.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Noack2021 parameterizes binary LATE monotonicity violations by defier share and outcome-distribution sensitivity. Our seed conjectures that ordinal per-rung defier caps alone can tighten the identified set because the same latent response path must satisfy all adjacent rungs simultaneously.

anchor_paper.author: Claudia Noack
anchor_paper.claim: Derives sensitivity bounds for LATE under binary-instrument monotonicity violations using defier-share and outcome-distribution parameters.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: this is not another binary sensitivity parameter; the mechanism is ordinal cross-rung compatibility absent in the binary setup.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Noack2021 because each adjacent rung is a binary IV problem.
seed_details.rebuttal: Solving adjacent binary problems independently relaxes the requirement that all rungs share one response path and one treatment effect per unit.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work BalkePearl1997 establishes sharp binary-IV bounds through a latent response-type LP. Our seed conjectures that the ordinal bounded-defier LP has new cross-rung facets because response paths 010 and 101 are impossible to decompose into independent binary principal strata.

anchor_paper.author: Alexander Balke and Judea Pearl
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Gives sharp binary-IV treatment-effect bounds via latent response-type linear programming.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: it predicts a strict new facet in the ordinal LP, not just applying the LP method to more categories.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from BalkePearl1997 by enlarging the response-type table.
seed_details.rebuttal: The LP existence is routine, but the pairwise non-pasteability and zig-zag facet are not characterized by the binary Balke-Pearl table.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work deChaisemartin2017 would imply defiers can be tolerated under compliers-defiers cancellation. Our seed conjectures that without cancellation, ordinal zig-zag paths generate partial-ID tightening rather than point identification because cancellation must be globally path-compatible.

anchor_paper.author: Clément de Chaisemartin
anchor_paper.claim: Shows IV remains valid under a weaker compliers-defiers condition than global monotonicity.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: it studies the failure mode when cancellation is absent and the remaining compatibility still tightens partial-ID bounds.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from deChaisemartin2017 because CD conditions already handle defiers.
seed_details.rebuttal: CD is a point-identifying equality condition; the proposed per-rung cap model does not impose cancellation and instead asks for the sharp interval.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work DahlHuberMellace2023 identifies LATEs under local monotonicity/local CD assumptions. Our seed conjectures that weaker per-rung defier caps yield a sharp interval with an ordinal kink because local defier roles overlap across adjacent pairs.

anchor_paper.author: Christian M. Dahl, Martin Huber, and Giovanni Mellace
anchor_paper.claim: Identifies LATEs on compliers and defiers under local monotonicity and local compliers-defiers assumptions.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: not weakening assumptions for its own sake; the weaker model reveals an ordinal kink/facet absent from their point-ID regime.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from DahlHuberMellace2023 because local CD already permits compliers and defiers.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their local assumptions identify alternative LATEs; per-rung caps alone leave an interval whose active constraints may be ordinal response-path compatibility.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work MogstadTorgovitskyWalters2021 studies positive 2SLS weights under partial monotonicity with multiple instruments. Our seed conjectures that a single ordinal instrument has an analogous but distinct compatibility test because adjacent monotonicity slack must come from one ordered response path.

anchor_paper.author: Magne Mogstad, Alexander Torgovitsky, and Christopher R. Walters
anchor_paper.claim: Characterizes conditions under which multiple-IV 2SLS has a positive-weight LATE interpretation under partial monotonicity.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: this is not extending multiple-IV 2SLS; it identifies a path-level compatibility phenomenon for one ordinal IV.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from partial monotonicity tests in MTW because ordinal rungs can be treated as instruments.
seed_details.rebuttal: The target is a sharp partial-ID bound, not a 2SLS positive-weight estimand, and the obstruction is shared zig-zag paths across adjacent rungs.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work BaiHuangMoonShaikhVytlacil2024 would predict generalized monotonicity may add no ATE identifying power. Our seed conjectures a latent-type LATE target behaves differently because the target denominator counts up-complier events whose path compatibility is restricted by defier caps.

anchor_paper.author: Yuehao Bai, Shunzhuang Huang, Sarah Moon, Azeem M. Shaikh, and Edward J. Vytlacil
anchor_paper.claim: Shows generalized monotonicity often has no identifying power for average potential outcomes and ATE beyond exogeneity.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Target-variation pattern, not blocked: the new target reveals a latent-denominator compatibility gap hidden in ATE formulations.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Bai et al. because monotonicity restrictions do not tighten average-effect sets.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their target is ATE/average potential outcomes; ordinal up-complier LATE conditions on response events and therefore depends on path compatibility.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work HuberMellace2015 and Kitagawa2015 use inequality violations to test LATE assumptions. Our seed conjectures that adjacent-rung inequality slack can be converted into sharp LATE bound width because slack identifies how much zig-zag mass can enter the response-path polytope.

anchor_paper.author: Martin Huber and Giovanni Mellace; Toru Kitagawa
anchor_paper.venue: Review of Economics and Statistics; Econometrica
anchor_paper.claim: Derive testable inequality implications of IV validity and LATE assumptions.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: it converts diagnostic slack into a sharp estimand-bound geometry, not just another validity test.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from inequality tests because they already characterize violations of LATE assumptions.
seed_details.rebuttal: Those tests reject or fail to reject; they do not optimize the ordinal LATE target over response-path-compatible bounded-defier laws.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Vytlacil2002 equates monotonicity with a latent-index selection model. Our seed conjectures that bounded defier caps reveal the first finite violation of scalar-index order because 010 and 101 paths create a measurable ordinal incompatibility facet.

anchor_paper.author: Edward Vytlacil
anchor_paper.claim: Establishes an equivalence between independence plus monotonicity and a latent-index selection model.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Boundary/regime-shift pattern, not blocked: the mechanism is a finite path-facet obstruction to scalar-index order.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Vytlacil2002 because any nonmonotone path is just a latent-index violation.
seed_details.rebuttal: The proposal quantifies partial identification after bounded violations, including how zig-zag paths tighten adjacent LATE bounds.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work AngristImbensRubin1996 decomposes binary IV principal strata. Our seed conjectures that ordinal principal strata are not just repeated binary strata because the same unit can be both a complier and a defier on different adjacent rungs.

anchor_paper.author: Joshua D. Angrist, Guido W. Imbens, and Donald B. Rubin
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Defines IV principal strata and LATE identification under random assignment, exclusion, and monotonicity.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Not blocked: it identifies a regime change in principal-stratum geometry, not a simple multi-level restatement.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from AIR1996 because ordinal strata are just longer treatment-response vectors.
seed_details.rebuttal: Longer vectors create overlap of complier and defier roles across adjacent rungs; binary principal-stratum algebra has no such compatibility constraint.

proposed_from.literature_map: ImbensAngrist1994, Econometrica/NBER, https://www.nber.org/papers/t0118: binary LATE is point identified under IV validity and no defiers; tension is full monotonicity. AngristImbens1995, JASA, https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1995.10476535: variable treatment intensity IV estimands are weighted causal responses under ordered monotonicity; open gap is calibrated partial defiance. AngristImbensRubin1996, JASA: principal strata make compliers/defiers explicit; phenomenon is latent response paths. Vytlacil2002, Econometrica, https://doi.org/10.1111/1468-0262.00282: monotonicity corresponds to latent-index selection; zig-zag paths violate scalar ordering. BalkePearl1997, JASA, https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1997.10474074: sharp IV bounds via latent response-type LP; gap is analytic ordinal response-path compatibility. deChaisemartin2017, Quantitative Economics, https://doi.org/10.3982/QE601: LATE can survive defiers under CD conditions; gap is partial ID under only per-rung caps. DahlHuberMellace2023, Econometrics Journal, https://doi.org/10.1093/ectj/utad013: local monotonicity/LCD identify LATEs with defiers; tension is weaker bounded-defier sensitivity. Noack2021, arXiv, https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.06421: defier-share sensitivity for LATE; gap is ordinal cross-rung compatibility beyond binary sensitivity. MogstadTorgovitskyWalters2021, AER, https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20190221: partial monotonicity for multiple IVs and positive 2SLS weights; related tension is local monotonicity diagnostics. BaiHuangMoonShaikhVytlacil2024, NBER, https://doi.org/10.3386/w32983: generalized monotonicity often has no ATE identifying power; contrast is latent-type LATE targets. HuberMellace2015 and Kitagawa2015 test LATE validity via inequalities; gap is converting monotonicity slack into sharp bounds.
